name: User Reviews
business_goal: Let shoppers make confident buying decisions through trustworthy reviews: only customers who actually purchased and received a product can rate it. Defaults are chosen to 'fail alive' — reviews publish and stay visible even in stores that never staff a moderation queue.
business_value: Reviews are table stakes: every mature storefront has them, and their absence loses adopters before Nimara's differentiators get evaluated. This epic closes that credibility gap and doubles as a reference implementation of the provider-swap architecture — the contract the primary Storefront Developer persona judges Nimara by. Verified social proof lifts product-page conversion, review structured data improves organic visibility, and rating aggregates give store operators direct quality feedback about their catalog.
primary_personas: Customer (verified buyer) — invited to review products after their order is fulfilled
Shopper (guest or logged-in) — reads reviews while deciding on a purchase
Store administrator — monitors published reviews, handles reports, moderates the queue when pre-moderation is enabled
Developer — adopts Nimara and needs to enable, configure, theme, and extend the reviews feature without forking
scope: ReviewProvider interface in the storefront: all review reads/writes go through a documented provider contract, so the default implementation can be swapped (e.g. for a SaaS review service) and survives a commerce-engine swap

Default implementation: a Nimara-owned reviews module with its own storage — engine-agnostic, supporting moderation queues, filtering, and rating aggregation (deliberately not commerce-engine metadata)
Review submission: 1–5 star rating and text comment, available only to customers whose fully fulfilled order contains the product (verified purchase); one review per product per order

Review invitation email sent after order fulfillment with a configurable delay (default 3 days); submission window configurable (default 30 days from fulfillment); email sent through a pluggable email-provider adapter — the invitation is the only notification in v1

Moderation modes, both shipped in v1: auto-publish with post-moderation (default — reviews publish immediately, reports pull them back into the queue, admins can unpublish) and pre-moderation (nothing public before approval) as a configuration switch
Standalone admin app for moderation: pending/reported queue, approve / reject / unpublish, filtering by rating and product, bulk actions
Reporting a published review as inappropriate — the review returns to the moderation queue
Product page: average rating, review count, paginated list of published reviews, filtering by star rating
Rating and review count displayed on product cards across catalog and discovery surfaces

Vendor-attributable schema: reviews carry an optional seller/vendor reference (populated when order lines have vendor attribution) and aggregates can group by vendor — no marketplace UX in this epic, but no schema migration when the marketplace initiative needs per-vendor ratings
Review structured data (schema markup) on product pages for SEO
Audit trail of all moderation actions
Configuration via environment/code (not hidden in UIs), documented: enable/disable, moderation mode, invitation delay, review window
out_of_scope: Photo attachments — deliberate fast-follow; deferring removes the upload pipeline, object-storage adapter, image compression, image moderation, and the associated privacy (GDPR) surface from v1
Reminder emails and moderation-outcome notifications (approved/rejected) — fast-follow once the core loop is proven
Automated content screening (profanity, spam detection) — future enhancement
Marketplace UX: per-vendor rating display, vendor dashboards, vendor replies (schema readiness only — see scope)
Importing or syndicating reviews from external platforms (Google, Trustpilot)
Incentives or rewards for writing reviews
Carrier-confirmed delivery as the verification trigger — fulfillment + delay is the deliberate proxy; tracking integrations are out
user_stories: As a customer, a few days after my order is fulfilled I receive an email inviting me to review the products I bought, so I can share my experience while it's fresh.
As a customer, I want to have ability to browse my reviews
As a customer, I can rate a product 1–5 stars and write a comment, and (by default) see it published immediately, so contributing feels worthwhile.
As a shopper, I see the average rating, number of reviews, and a paginated, filterable review list on the product page, so I can make an informed decision.
As a shopper, I can report an inappropriate review, so harmful content gets pulled back for re-checking.
As a store administrator, I can unpublish any review and work a queue of reported (or, in pre-moderation mode, pending) reviews with filters and bulk actions.
As a store administrator, I see rating summaries per product, so I can spot quality issues in the catalog.

As a developer, I can enable or disable reviews and adjust settings (moderation mode, invitation delay, review window) through documented configuration, so I can fit the feature to my store without forking the code.
As a developer, review UI components are composable and themeable, and all review data flows through the ReviewProvider contract, so I can restyle the experience or swap the backing implementation.
acceptance_criteria: A review can be submitted only by a customer whose fully fulfilled order contains the product; one review per product per order.
The invitation email is sent automatically after the configured delay from fulfillment; submission is possible only within the configured window, counted from fulfillment.
In auto-publish mode (default), a submitted review is publicly visible immediately; in pre-moderation mode, no review is visible before approval — switching modes is a single configuration change.
Reporting a published review returns it to the moderation queue without deleting it; an administrator's unpublish takes effect immediately.
The moderation queue supports approve, reject, unpublish, filtering by rating and product, and bulk actions.
The product page shows average rating, review count, and a paginated list with star-rating filtering; product cards across the storefront show average rating and count.
All storefront review reads/writes go through the ReviewProvider interface; the default module is the only component with direct access to review storage.
Review records store an optional vendor/seller reference when order lines carry vendor attribution, and aggregates can be computed per vendor.
Published reviews are exposed as review structured data on product pages.
Every moderation action is recorded in the audit trail.
Enable/disable and all settings (moderation mode, invitation delay, review window) are configurable via environment/code and documented; invitation email template is customizable.
risks: The reviews module is the first stateful component in an otherwise stateless storefront stack — it adds a database and migrations to the Zero-to-Deploy story, the roadmap's #1 priority; the deploy path must absorb it invisibly.

A standalone admin app is heavy for a single workflow: it carries its own auth, deployment, and maintenance, and risks becoming a dead-end tool if the broader console question (see open questions) is answered differently later.

Stores that opt into pre-moderation but never staff the queue silently kill the feature — reviews pile up unpublished; mitigated by the auto-publish default, but the pre-moderation path needs queue-size visibility.
Auto-publish default means an occasional bad review is public before an admin reacts — recoverable via report/unpublish, but stores must know the switch exists.
Low review volume (single invitation email, no reminders or incentives in v1) → weak social proof, especially on new products.
Review invitations depend on stores reliably marking orders fulfilled — stores with sloppy fulfillment data get distorted invitation timing and windows.
open_questions: Standalone admin app: is it the seed of a general Nimara operator console (auth, roles, navigation designed for reuse by future workflows like invoicing and branding) or a reviews-only tool? Deliberately deferred — must be decided before implementation starts, not implicitly by the first commit.
Photo fast-follow: target release, storage-provider abstraction, and image-moderation approach when photos are added.
Should v1 ship a second ReviewProvider adapter (e.g. a SaaS review service) to prove the contract, or is the interface + default module enough?
What queue-size visibility or alerting do pre-moderation stores need so an unstaffed queue is noticed?
GDPR: how are reviews handled when a customer requests account deletion — anonymize (keep content) or delete?
